Chunks index updated: 2026-06-09 20:10 UTC

Searching vault… Searching transcripts… Nike was founded by Phil Knight and Bill Bowerman. Phil Knight was a University of Oregon track athlete and Stanford MBA who, in 1964, started the company (originally called Blue Ribbon Sports) importing Japanese running shoes. His former track coach at Oregon, Bill Bowerman, became his co-founder — the obsessive tinkerer and design mind who famously created the waffle sole by pouring rubber into his wife's waffle iron in their kitchen [ep-0010] [ep-0153]. The partnership was essentially: Knight handled the business (selling shoes out of his trunk in the early days, often dismissed by his father as "jackassing around" [ep-0010]), while Bowerman relentlessly pursued marginal engineering gains. Bowerman's core philosophy was that lightness = speed. He believed every ounce shaved from a shoe translated directly to competitive advantage, and that obsessive pursuit became embedded in Nike's DNA [ep-0010]. An ironic coda: Bowerman's decades of working with rubber cement in a poorly ventilated workshop caused permanent nerve damage in his legs. He ultimately couldn't run in the shoes he spent his life perfecting [ep-0153].

tool_rounds.reasoning:
**Nike was founded by Phil Knight and Bill Bowerman.**

Phil Knight was a University of Oregon track athlete and Stanford MBA who, in 1964, started the company (originally called Blue Ribbon Sports) importing Japanese running shoes. His former track coach at Oregon, Bill Bowerman, became his co-founder — the obsessive tinkerer and design mind who famously created the waffle sole by pouring rubber into his wife's waffle iron in their kitchen [ep-0010] [ep-0153].

The partnership was essentially: Knight handled the business (selling shoes out of his trunk in the early days, often dismissed by his father as "jackassing around" [ep-0010]), while Bowerman relentlessly pursued marginal engineering gains. Bowerman's core philosophy was that **lightness = speed**. He believed every ounce shaved from a shoe translated directly to competitive advantage, and that obsessive pursuit became embedded in Nike's DNA [ep-0010].

An ironic coda: Bowerman's decades of working with rubber cement in a poorly ventilated workshop caused permanent nerve damage in his legs. He ultimately **couldn't run in the shoes he spent his life perfecting** [ep-0153].

turns.action: send 'What book did he rely on?'
turns.response_text: Searching vault… Searching transcripts… Searching vault (parallel)… Loading episode… Searching transcripts… Searching vault… I've dug through the vault extensively on this — across multiple search angles — and here's the honest answer: the vault doesn't identify a specific book that Phil Knight relied on as his guiding text. This is actually revealing in itself. The "book" that set Nike in motion wasn't something Knight read — it was something he *wrote*. While at Stanford's Graduate School of Business in 1962, Knight wrote a paper titled: "Can Japanese Sports Shoes Do to German Sports Shoes What Japanese Cameras Did to German Cameras?" [ep-0022]. That paper was the blueprint. Japanese cameras had disrupted German dominance (Leica, etc.) by being cheaper and just as good — Knight saw the same dynamic waiting to happen in athletic footwear, with Japanese brands poised to undercut Adidas and Puma. His classmates were reportedly bored by the presentation [ep-0022]. He went and built the thesis anyway. Beyond that, Knight's philosophical framework in *Shoe Dog* is highly self-generated — the core insight he returns to isn't from a book but from a moment of clarity on a morning run: "Play. Yes, I thought, that's it. That's the word. The secret of happiness..." [ep-0010]. His drive wasn't book-derived; it was experiential. One thing the vault *does* surface indirectly: Knight loved books broadly ("The things I love most, books, sports, democracy, free enterprise started as crazy ideas" [ep-0010]), but unlike many founders in this archive who explicitly name a single book that shaped them (Bezos with *The Goal*, for instance [ep-0179]), Knight's references remain autobiographical rather than external. If there's a specific book he singled out, the vault simply doesn't have it.
